head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots interfacial-decorated hierarchical Ni/PS core/shell nanocapsules for tunable microwave absorption

ce:section.ce:para: Electromagnetic interference (EMI) pollution has become a very serious problem in recent years since the development and miniaturization of the electronic and communication devices [1–4]. Many kinds of microwave absorption materials have been prepared to reduce the EMI pollution [5–12]. Core/shell-nanostructured microwave absorbers provide a potential and promising solution with a magnetic/dielectric assembly, respectively taking charge of the magnetic and dielectric attenuations. The interfacial polarizations, impedance matching, multiple scattering etc. of the combinations also have great effects on the interfacial reflection, effective absorbing bandwidth, and microwave absorption performance, referring to the key properties and features of the new generation microwave absorbing materials [13–15]. Many kinds of core/shell composites have been reported involving unique nanostructures, lightweight fitting, and enhanced performances [16–22].

Core/shell-nanostructured absorbers are regarded as an effective and perspective way to achieve the broadband microwave absorption [23–25]. However, most reported cases always meet an unbalanced electromagnetic (EM) impedance with a relative low permeability corresponding to a relative high permittivity [26]. Great interfacial reflection will be generated for the incident microwave due to the transmission-line theory [27]. Lots of composites have been successfully prepared to improve the input impedance using different core/shell or core/shell/shell nanostructures and nanoarchitectures, including FeNi3/SiO2/rGO-PANI [28], Co/C [29], Fe3O4/C [30], Fe3O4/TiO2 [31], MnFe2O4/TiO2 [32] etc. The adjustment and equilibrium of the EM parameters in the core/shell absorbers have become the main focus in the microwave absorption performance.

Very recently, shells of 2D-material have been frequently employed to couple with the magnetic cores for enhanced microwave absorbing properties (e.g. graphene, MoS2, g-C3N4, MXene, etc.) [33–36]. Sharp promotion of absorption arises as the reinforce of dielectric polarizations brought from the large specific surface area, high carrier mobility, and excellent electrical property of 2D layered nanostructures [36]. However, strong absorption can only exhibit in several narrow bands owing to the bad impedance. In our recent paper, the conductivity of the polymer shells has been proved to tremendously affect the permittivity by the charge behaviors and interfacial polarizations on the interfaces [37]. From a view of dielectric attenuation, proper improvement for the microwave sensitivity of interfacial dipoles through an organic-inorganic composited way maybe a good option for the permittivity adjustment of the 2D-material based core/shell absorbers, which gives a great offer for the tunable microwave absorbers.

The elevation of permeability is also a common problem in the nanoscaled core/shell absorbers. Many works tried to improve the permeability and magnetic loss by unique hollow or hierarchical nanoarchitectures of magnetic metals, alloys, and ferrites to achieve a multiple reflection and exceed the Sneok’s limit [16,26]. 1D and 2D-nanostructures have exhibited higher permeability than the spherical or cubic one owing to the shape and magneto-crystalline anisotropy. Several nanostructures have been synthesized in our previous works (Ni hexagonal nanoflakes [38], ZnFe2O4 nanoflakes [39,40], and Ni nanochains [37]) with broad absorbing bandwidth and enhanced microwave absorption, because of the ordered easy magnetization and interfacial polarizations. Thus, nanoarchitecture design proposes a possibility for the effective increasing of permeability in the core/shell absorbers.

In this study, we prepared the magnetic/dielectric Ni/GQDs/PS (NGP) core/shell nanocapsules assembled by the hierarchical Ni magnetic core and the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) decorated polystyrene (PS) dielectric shell using three-step liquid chemical processes The hierarchically nanoarchitectured Ni cores were constituted by many randomly intersected Ni nanoflakes in order to enhance the magnetic attenuation of microwave by shape-anisotropy. The GQDs as a non-zero bandgap semiconductor were decorated on the interface aiming to appropriately strengthen the dielectric attenuation by the intrinsic polarization of defects and the interfacial polarization of functional groups [41,42]. The PS shell was coated on the as-decorated Ni/GQDs (NG) core for a tunable dielectric property and EM impedance, depending on the weakening effect of dipolar polarization by the insulative amorphous polymer. Shells with various ratio of GQDs/PS in the NGP nanocapsules were synthesized and studied for an investigation of dielectric attenuation tunability via the combination of polar and non-polar contents. The frequency (f) dependence of microwave EM properties, impedance matching maps, reflection loss (RL) and simulations in 1–18 GHz were also evaluated to explain the interfacial polarizations between core/shell induced tunable dielectric properties, EM impedance, and effective absorption performance.

All the reagents were of analytical grade and purchased commercially from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., Aladdin Bio-Chem Tech. Co. Ltd., and Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech. Co. Ltd. in China without any further purification. Nickel acetate tetrahydrate (NiAc, Ni(CH3COO)2·4H2O), ethylene glycol (EG), polyethylene glycol (PEG, Mw. = 4000), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were used to synthesize the hierarchical Ni nanocores. Water-soluble graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs, 1 mg/mL) and hydrazine hydrate (N2H4·H2O) were employed to reduce the decorated GQDs on the interfaces. Styrene and ammonium persulfate (APS, (NH4)2S2O8) were adopted to prepare the PS shell on the as-decorated core. Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, K30), oleic acid (OA), and sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate (SDBS) were chosen for a core/shell interfacial treatment and modification. Anhydrous ethanol and deionized (DI) water were used as the solvent for dissolution and washing.

The hierarchical Ni nanoarchitectures were fabricated by an alkali-assisted solvothermal method. Briefly, NiAc of 3 mmol and PEG of 1 g was dissolved in EG of 20 mL to yield a transparent solution under magnetic stirring at 80 °C for 15 min. Meanwhile, an alkaline solution was prepared by dissolving NaOH of 48 mmol into EG of 10 mL under constant stirring at 80 °C. Then, the settled EG solution containing Ni2+ was mixed up with the alkaline EG solution slowly by a rapid stir until complete precipitation. The obtained homogeneous mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated in an oven at 200 °C for 8 h. The Ni product was separated by a magnet and washed ultrasonically by ethanol and DI water for several times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Decoration of GQDs on Ni cores
ce:section.ce:para: The decoration of GQDs on the Ni nanoarchitectures was operated using a facile low-temperature reduction process. In brief, the as-prepared Ni nanoarchitectures of 0.2 g were dispersed in 100 mL DI water. PVP of 1 g was added in the mixture with a continuous ultrasonic treatment for 2 h. Then, the Ni nanoarchitectures were separated out magnetically and re-dispersed homogeneously into a solution containing DI water of 45 mL and water-soluble GOQDs of 9 mg under mechanical stirring. This mixture was transferred into a four-necked flask equipped with a mechanical stirrer, a condenser, a thermometer, a fabric heating mantle, inlet and outlet tubes for nitrogen (N2) gas, aiming to provide an inert and antioxidative atmosphere for the reduction reaction. Under a highly purified N2 gas flow, N2H4·H2O of 4 mL was gradually injected into the mixture under mechanical stirring at 95 °C for 4 h. The resultant NG product was separated magnetically and washed ultrasonically by ethanol and DI water.

The PS shell coating on the as-prepared NG product was proceeded using an in-situ polymerization method [37]. Typically, the as-prepared NG product was first sonicated at 65 °C in a solution of 30 mL ethanol and 1 mL OA. The pretreated NG product was then separated by a magnet and dispersed into another solution containing SDBS of 0.03 g and DI water of 45 mL. Styrene was added into the above NG mixture under ultrasonication at room temperature (RT). This precursor was transformed to a four-necked flask and heated under constant mechanical stirring and high purity N2 atmosphere at 70 °C for 4 h. The polymerization was initialized by slowly injecting the APS into the precursor. The resultant NGP product was separated magnetically, washed with ethanol and DI water for several times, and dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h. Shells with different ratio of GQDs/PS were synthesized and coated on the Ni cores in order to investigate the tunability of dielectric attenuation properties in the polar/nonpolar combined shell. For a clear description, the NGP products synthesized using the same amount of NG cores and different amount of PS shells were labeled as NGP-1, NGP-2, NGP-3, and NGP-4, corresponding to the initial reagents of styrene in 0.025 mL, 0.05 mL, 0.075 mL, 0.1 mL, and APS in 0.02 g, 0.03 g, 0.05 g, 0.06 g, respectively.

The structures of the as-prepared Ni, NG and NGP products was analyzed by a θ-2θ scan using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Panalytical X’Pert Pro MPD) with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å). The morphology of nanoarchitectures and the high-resolution core/shell interfaces was observed by a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Hitachi SU8000) with the emission voltage of 30 kV and a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F) with the emission voltage of 200 kV. The complex relative permittivity (ε
r = ε

The as-prepared products for EM property measurements were fabricated by mixing up with paraffin wax homogeneously in a weight percentage of 40%, and pressing into a ring sample by a toroidal mold with an outer diameter of 7 mm, an inner diameter of 3.04 mm, and a thickness of 2 mm. The RL was carefully analyzed to evaluate the microwave absorbing properties of the paraffin-bonded samples, which was calculated according to the transmission line theory using Eqs. (1) and (2):

Fig. 1 shows the XRD θ-2θ patterns of the as-prepared Ni, NG, and NGP products. The angular positions of the diffraction peaks at 2θ = 44.5°, 51.8°, 76.4° in all six products are matched well with the (111), (200) and (220) planes of a typical face-centered cubic (f.c.c.) Ni phase, according to JCPDS 04-0850 [44]. No diffraction relates to additional impurities, such as NiO, Ni(OH)2 etc., implying the high purity of the f.c.c. Ni phase in these products [45]. Besides, broad peaks with the center at 2θ = 23.7° may be a characteristic feature of the amorphous PS phase or the GQDs in NG and NGP [33,46]. Fig. 2 (a) shows the SEM image of the uncoated Ni product. It can be seen that the Ni product presents a hierarchical nanoarchitectured morphology with the average particle size of ∼1 μm. The nanoarchitectures own accordant shapes constructed by several randomly intersected nanoflakes of ∼500 nm. Fig. 2(b) is the TEM image of the typical NGP-2 product. Based on the same hierarchical morphology of the Ni core with a dark contrast, the NGP product shows an obvious core/shell nanostructure of ∼1.2 μm with a uniform light contrast shell of ∼35 nm. The magnified TEM image of the NGP-2 core/shell interfaces shown in Fig. 2(c) exhibits that the amorphous PS shell on the Ni core is decorated homogeneously by many tiny nanocrystallites with the average size of 3–5 nm assigning to the GQDs. The HRTEM image of the shell in Fig. 2(d) distinctly illustrates the crystalline GQDs of periodic lattice fringes dispersing in the disordered amorphous PS phase with compact interfaces.

The anisotropic growth of Ni in an alkaline solution by a hydrothermal method has been carefully discussed in our previous report [38]. In our case here, the Ni2+ identically reacted with OH− to precipitate the Ni(OH)2 as the formula in Eq. (3).

However, as the existence of the long-chain PEG, the complexed Ni(OH)2 precipitation should be in the state of isotropic clusters to keep the lowest energy. Under the reducing action of EG, the Ni embryos generate following the reaction of Eqs. (4) and (5) [47].

As the gradual growth of embryos, the Ni nanoparticle will reach a critical state from an internal growth in the cluster to an outspread growth on the rough surface [48]. With the increment of H2O as the intermediate and the consumption of NaOH by neutralization of organic acids, the reducing growth rate of Ni begins to slow down and offers an anisotropic growth along the higher energy plane instead of the hasty isotropic growth to equilibrate the surface free energy. Besides, the growth of easy magnetization direction is also preferred in favor of the lowest magnetic energy. For a f.c.c. Ni lattice, the surface free energy of planes can be sorted as γ{111}<γ{100}<γ{110} [49], while the easy magnetization direction is <111> [50]. Thus, with the stimulation of alkaline circumstance, the humps on the Ni nuclei surface should preferentially and competitively tend to grow along the <110> and <111>, or the <100> and <111> direction to finally form the hierarchical Ni nanoarchitectures with randomly intersected 2D-nanoflakes.

Fig. 3 (a and b) shows the f dependence of the real (ε
r ′) and imaginary (ε
r ″) part in the complex permittivity of Ni, NG, and NGP paraffin-bonded samples in 1–18 GHz (L–Ku), respectively. The Ni sample exhibits a relatively strong ε
r in a stable trend with ε
r ′ of ∼8.17 and ε
r ″ of ∼0.84, and a small relaxation at 11.12 GHz, due to the shape-anisotropy induced in-plane intrinsic polarization enhancement and out-of-plane interfacial polarization [38]. Compared with Ni, the ε
r of NG sample has a tremendous increment in ε
r ′ of ∼53% and ε
r ″ of ∼102%, indicating the abundant polarizations of the GQDs derived from the defects induced dipoles during the GOQDs reducing reaction [41]. With the coating of PS, all of the NGP samples display an obvious decrement of both ε
r ′ and ε
r ″ in the whole band. Since the electronic, ionic, and atomic polarizations can be neglected in a very weak applied dynamic EM field, intrinsic polarizations from lattice dipoles and interfacial polarizations from interfacial dipoles should play an important role in a core/shell nanocapsule absorber. In our case, a basic level of ε
r has been brought by the relatively high polarizations of Ni and GQDs in the NGP samples. The nonpolar PS shell has much less charges-induced dipoles and weaker contribution for the ε
r because of the insulative covalent-bond structure [37]. However, in the reaction of polymerization, the water soluble GQDs on the Ni core should diffuse in the EG and decorate in the PS shell, as shown in Fig. 2(c and d). Then, the insulative PS is inserted between Ni/GQDs and GQD/GQD that effectively obstructs their conductive connections and weakens the interfacial polarizations, resulting the decrement of ε
r . Interestingly, the curves reveal an obvious tunability in both ε
r ′ and ε
r ″ with the decline percentage of 36%, 38%, 44% and 53% as the PS ratio increase from NGP-1 to NGP-4, respectively. It implies the gradual weakening effect of the interfacial dipolar polarizations as the distance between Ni/GQDs and GQD/GQD becomes larger. Fig. 3(c) is the Cole-Cole plots of Ni, NG, and NGP samples based on ε
r ′ and ε
r ″. The semicircles in the plots suggest the typical Debye’s dielectric relaxations, according to Debye’s theory described by,
(6) (ε
r ′ − ε
∞ ) 2 + (ε
r ″) 2 = (ε
s − ε
∞ ) 2
where ε
∞ and ε
s are the optical and the stationary dielectric constant, respectively. Compared with the small relaxation in Ni, no obvious dielectric relaxation shown in the NG sample infers the ordered out-of-plane dipoles on the Ni nanoflakes are disturbed by the GQDs contact. With the coating of PS in NGP samples, the big semicircles on behalf of the relaxations referring to the interfacial dipoles are activated since the conductive aggregation state are improved by PS. Compared with the relaxation of NGP-1 sample located at 10.79 GHz, the relaxations in NGP-2, NGP-3, and NGP-4 samples exhibit a gradually blue-shift to 11.02, 11.24. and 12.73 GHz, respectively. This pattern illustrates that thin PS layer between Ni/GQDs and GQD/GQD may has a certain effect on the rotation of interfacial dipoles under microwave EM field. As the PS layer becomes thicker, interfacial dipoles depending on the charges emission are greatly weakened by the further distance between interfaces, giving rise to a difficult response to the microwave with a blue-shift of the relaxation.

Fig. 4 (a and b) shows the f dependence of the real (μ
r ′) and imaginary (μ
r ″) part in the complex permeability of Ni, NG, and NGP paraffin-bonded samples in 1–18 GHz (L–Ku), respectively. The permeability spectra remain a relative high level due to the shape-anisotropy of the hierarchical Ni cores. All the samples exhibit the same gradual downward trend as the increase of f, and the characteristic dual-resonance on account of the traditional static magnetic energy of Ni in S band at 2.2 and 2.8 GHz [37]. The coincident μ
r spectra indicates the magnetic attenuation in the uncoated and coated samples are mainly originated from the Ni cores. The negative parts of μ
r ″ for Ni in 15.9–18 GHz and NG in 14.8–18 GHz should be assigned to the magnetic gain induced by the microwave current generated magnetic field in the conductive matrix without PS [38][39]. We compare the μ
r spectra with several previous studies involving isotropic Ni nanostructures, as shown in Table 1 . The hierarchical Ni nanoarchitectures in our case illustrate relatively higher value in both μ
r ′ and μ
r ″ owing to the shape-anisotropic nanoflakes induced easy magnetization enhancement. Microwave magnetic attenuation generally derives from hysteresis loss, domain-wall resonance, the eddy-current effect, and natural resonance [51,52]. For the hierarchical Ni nanoarchitectures composed by nanoflakes here, the magnetic hysteresis loss and the domain-wall resonance can be neglected in nanoscale and weak applied field. The eddy-current loss caused by the skin-effect is also proved to be absent in our case with insulative PS coating and paraffin embedding, because the value of μ
r ″(μ
r ′)−2 f −1 is not equal to a constant of 2πμ 0 d 2 σ as the variation of f in Fig. 4(c). Hence, the natural resonance is still the primary mechanism, and the preferred growth of the nanoflakes in Ni along the easy magnetization direction is benefit to the magnetic attenuation [52,53].

Fig. 5 (a and b) is the dielectric loss tangent (tanδ
ε = ε
r ″/ε
r ’) and magnetic loss tangent (tanδ
μ = μ
r ″/μ
r ′) of Ni, NG, and NGP paraffin-bonded samples respectively, which are calculated by the measured values of ε
r
’, ε
r ″, μ
r ′ and μ
r ″ in Figs. 3 and 4 with the f range of 1–18 GHz. As shown in Fig. 5(a), the tanδ
ε of the NG sample is much higher and sustainedly increasing at the whole L–Ku band, compared with Ni in an average increment of ∼58% and maximum of 131%. The extreme increment of dielectric loss is because the electron transportation has been improved by the decoration of GQDs on the surface of Ni based on the free-electron theory [37]. With the coating of insulative PS shell, the tanδ
ε curves of all the NGP samples emerge a great decrement and have a continuously decreasing trend as the increment of the PS ratio, except the peaks in X–Ku band corresponding to the interfacial dipolar relaxations. Compare with NGP-1, the decrement of NGP-2 and NGP-3 are relatively small with an average level of ∼0.1% and ∼3% respectively, which mainly focus on the band of L–S and Ku. A large percentage decrement of 36% appeared in NGP-4 sample indicates a critical point that the insulative PS has restrained the movement of charges between GQDs on the interfaces and weakened the interfacial polarization as the increasing ratio of PS content. The tanδ
μ of the six samples exhibit nearly identical level and similar trend in Fig. 5(b) inferring the basic magnetic attenuation from the Ni cores. The slightly negative tanδ
μ in NG sample demonstrates a magnetic gain deriving from the GQDs-decorated conductive connection resulted EM energy transformation effect [39].

In order to have a visual view of the polar/nonpolar shell effects on the input impedance of the core/shell nanocapsules, the 2D-contour maps of the f and d dependent Z in for Ni, NG, and NGP samples are respectively displayed in 1–18 GHz, as shown in Fig. 6 (a–f). According to Eq. (1), the closer the Z in approaches to 1, the less EM wave is reflected on the surface of absorber. Therefore, the zones of Z in = 0.8–1.2 are marked on the 2D-contour maps for a clear description. The Z in map of Ni shown in Fig. 6(a) presents hyperbolic shaped zones located from low-f/thick-d to high-f/thin-d. The main matching zones appears covering the whole Ku band with d of 4.4–7.3 mm except another two narrow belts. After the decoration of GQDs, the matching zone in Ku band is apparently vanished as shown in Fig. 6(b) and the belts become more tighter, owing to the tremendous increment of ε
r by the GQDs. With the PS coating, the matching zones in Ku band are recovered and broadened with d of 4.1–6.8, 4.2–7.0, 4.5–7.6, and 4.7–8 mm in the NGP-1, -2, -3, and -4 samples respectively, but Z in is getting worse gradually with the color of the center zone from green (matching) in NGP-1 to red in NGP-3 until the white (mismatching) in NGP-4, as shown in Fig. 6(c–f). Two new zones emerge in NGP-4 of green color in 8.5–11.9 GHz (X band) with d of 7–10 mm and red color in 15.5–18 GHz (Ku band) with d of 8.2–10 mm. Besides, the dual hyperbolic shaped zones become loose gradually and shifting towards the direction of thicker d with a blue-shifting step assigning to the interfacial dipolar relaxation. These illustrate a tunability of Z in in Ku band for the core/shell nanocapsule with a GQDs/PS polar/nonpolar shell through a tunable dielectric property by adjusting the distribution density of GQDs in the PS shell.

Fig. 7 shows the 2D-contour maps of f and d dependent RL for Ni, NG, and NGP paraffin-bonded samples in f of 1–18 GHz and the d of 0–10 mm, respectively. These maps are calculated by the measured ε
r and μ
r at various d using Eqs. (1) and (2). The hierarchical Ni nanoarchitecture shows a notably strong RL performance in X–Ku band in Fig. 7(a), compare with other reports referring to the materials of single component [6–12,16]. The intersected nanoflakes of Ni core highlight the rotation of magnetic moments along the easy magnetization direction with shape-anisotropy under microwave and improve the impedance matching, which gives rise to an effective absorbing bandwidth (RL < −10 dB) of 1.7 GHz at d = 4.8 mm and a minimum RL of −50.4 dB at f = 17.45 GHz and d = 4.7 mm in accordance with the impedance matching zone in Fig. 6(a). With GQDs decoration, the RL of NG sample in Fig. 7(b) has an apparent decrement in Ku band with d > 3.7 mm. The absorptions in L–C band with d > 2.4 mm and X–Ku band with d > 1.2 mm are strengthened reversely in a very narrow tolerance of d. The minimum RL drops to −20.03 dB at f = 18 GHz and d = 1.3, while the effective absorbing bandwidth is 2.72 GHz at d = 1.5 mm. It demonstrates the extremely high ε
r of GQDs results in a bad impedance (Fig. 6(b)) and greatly weakens the RL in the NG sample. Τhe RL of NGP samples with different GQDs/PS ratio in the shells exhibits improved absorption performance in X and Ku band comparing with Ni and NG as shown in Fig. 7(c–f). The NGP-1 in Fig. 7(c) presents three primary effective absorbing areas respectively arise in the f range of 4.6–11.5, 7.2–18, and 13.1–18 GHz and d range of 2.2–5.4, 4.3–10, and 7.2–10 mm. The minimum RL value reaches −44.3 dB at f = 14.04 GHz and d = 5.8 mm, while the effective absorption bandwidth is broadened to 2.08 GHz at d = 7.6 mm. The enhancement of RL at X and Ku band reveals that a proper depression of ε
r by shell coating has a positive effect on the impedance matching and performance intensifying for the original absorption of Ni. As the ascending of PS ratio in NGP-2, and -3, the RL of absorbing area in X band appears strengthening until falling back in NGP-4, while the RL in Ku band seems to descend and narrow continuously with d thickening. Although the minimum RL increases with −42.5 dB (f = 13.58 GHz, d = 6.1 mm), −44.3 dB (f = 12.9 GHz, d = 6.9 mm), and −52.6 dB (f = 10.13 GHz, d = 9 mm) for NGP-2, -3 and -4 respectively, the effective absorbing bandwidths are tightening and shifting towards thick-d with 2.34 GHz (d = 7.6 mm), 2.04 GHz (d = 7.8 mm), and 2.33 GHz (d = 7.6 mm). We compare the performance of the NGP-3 sample to several works in previous reports, including some absorbers of single component [6–12,16], and various composites (Table 2 ) [5, 13, 16, 18, 19-22]. The NGP-3 exhibits a better minimum RL value and equivalent absorbing bandwidth in X–Ku band. It demonstrates the great effect of dielectric regulation on the microwave absorption properties using the GQDs interfacial-decoration in PS [58].

In order to evaluate the microwave absorption efficiency of the NGP nanocapsule samples with different GQDs/PS ratio, the microwave absorption efficiencies (R
E ) of two primary strong absorbing areas at various d in Fig. 7(c–f) are respectively calculated using the f dependent RL curves of NGP-1, -2, -3, and -4 and presented in Fig. 8 and Table 3 by Eqs. (7) and (8) [45],
(7)
R E
= Δ S / d
(8)
Δ S =
∫
R L d f
where ΔS is the shaded area below the dashed line of RL = −10 dB. The shaded areas of the effective absorbing peaks at f of ∼11 GHz and d of ∼2.6 exhibits an obvious increasing trend from NGP-1 to -3 with the corresponding R
E value of 47.98, 60.43, and 83.7 dB GHz/mm respectively, as shown in Fig. 8(a–c), until a sharp decline in NGP-4 with R
E of 11.17 dB GHz/mm and f blue-shifting to 12.5 GHz in Fig. 8(d). The rise-fall trend is similarly repeated in another absorbing area with d > 5 mm. As the PS ratio increase, the R
E value of the strongest peak with similar RL level ascends from NGP-1 to -2, and then descends from NGP-3 to -4 with the value of 45.93, 49.67, 34.51, and 30.83 dB GHz/mm respectively, while the position red-shifts towards low-f labeled by the red paint in Fig. 8(a–d). These tendencies infer a critical point of performance in the ratio around NGP-2 and -3, and an apparent tunable microwave absorption in X–Ku band, deriving from the tunable ε
r and impedance.

The mechanism of microwave absorption properties is mainly decided by the dielectric/magnetic relaxations in a core/shell-nanostructured system [40]. The contributions of the dielectric and magnetic attenuation for the microwave, as well as their interaction, are respectively simulated and revealed for the NGP-3 sample in Fig. 9 [12,59–62]. Compared with the RL in 3D-contour map and curves of NGP-3 in Fig. 9(a and b), the f and d dependent RL was recalculated assuming ε
cal ′′ = 0 and ε
cal ′ is equal to the average ε
r ′ in accordance with Kramers-Kronig relations to simulate the microwave absorption with no dielectric action, as shown in Fig. 9(c and d). This assumption is logical because the ε
r ′ value of NGP-3 is steady within 5.87–7.89 in the f range of 1–18 GHz. The effective absorbing zone extremely shrinks into only two small areas in 7–14 GHz with the minimum RL of −20.0 dB. This implies the tremendous contribution of dielectric attenuation on the performance. The RL simulation of NGP-3 sample with no magnetic action is also analyzed by defining the magnetic susceptibility with χ
m = μ
r
/μ
0 -1 = 0, as shown in Fig. 9(e and f). The graph of 3D-contour map obviously illustrates the multiple absorbing peaks in Fig. 9(e). New peaks and enhancement are exhibited in the X and Ku band as variation of d in Fig. 9(f). The minimum RL can only reach −33.7 dB at a thick d of 9.2 mm, which also indicates the important contribution of magnetic attenuation and E/M interaction. It also highlights the significant role of dielectric regulation on the tunability of microwave absorption.

GQDs interfacial-decorated hierarchical Ni/PS core/shell nanocapsules, as well as the uncoated and as-decorated Ni nanoarchitectures, have been prepared by three-step liquid chemical processes, aiming to study the tunable permittivity deduced impedance and microwave absorption by the anisotropic core and polar/nonpolar shell. Based on the nanoflakes intersected Ni core with shape-anisotropy enhanced intrinsic polarization and natural resonance under microwave, the dielectric properties and attenuation can be flexibly tuned by adjusting the interfacial polarizations through a decoration way of the distance regulation between GQDs in the insulative PS shell. This strategy triggers a chain response and improvement of EM impedance and microwave absorption performance, especially in X and Ku band with a rise-fall pattern. A critical point is exhibited in the NGP-3 sample with the best microwave absorption efficiencies of 83.7 dB GHz/mm at the thickness of 2.7 mm. The minimum RL reaches −44.3 dB at 12.09 GHz with the thickness of 6.9 mm in NGP-3 and the effective absorption bandwidth (RL < −10dB) is up to 2.04 GHz at d = 7.8 mm. This study offers experience and possibility of designing core/shell-nanostructured absorbers with tunable microwave absorption properties through the dielectric attenuation regulation by functional shells for the broadband and lightweight microwave absorbers.
